title: Head & Neck Cancer — Overview
oneLiner: SCC of upper aerodigestive tract; smoking/HPV-driven; airway compromise is immediate threat.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway obstruction** from tumour or post-biopsy oedema: listen for stridor, check for biphasic stridor, distress, oxygen saturations. **Single finding:** stridor at rest with inability to lie flat.
- **Carotid blowout** (salvage after radiotherapy/surgery): sentinel bleed, then torrential haemorrhage. **Single finding:** sudden bright red blood from mouth/neck with hypotension.
sections.Presentation:
- Hoarseness >3 weeks, persistent sore throat, dysphagia, odynophagia, referred otalgia (ear pain).
- Neck lump (often cervical lymphadenopathy), non-healing oral ulcer >3 weeks, unilateral nasal obstruction/epistaxis.
- Exam: inspect oral cavity (ulcer, mass, leukoplakia/erythroplakia), palpate neck nodes (fixed/matted), check tongue movement & bite block (trismus), cranial nerves (XII, XI, X — tongue/shoulder/voice), flexible nasendoscopy bedside.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Benign laryngeal lesion** (nodules, papilloma) — bilateral, no ulcer, resolves with voice rest.
- **Infectious pharyngitis/tonsillitis** — acute onset, fever, bilateral exudate, lymphadenopathy tender.
- **Thyroid mass** — midline, moves with swallowing, no mucosal lesion.
- **Lymphoma** — rubbery neck nodes, B symptoms (night sweats, weight loss).
- **Splitter:** Unilateral tonsillar enlargement with ulceration → SCC until proven otherwise; bilateral symmetric enlargement with exudate → infectious.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Flexible nasal endoscopy (FNE) — assess airway patency, lesion site, vocal cord mobility. If stridor → do NOT delay: call ENT/anaesthetics.
- **Bloods:** FBC (anaemia, infection), U&E, LFT (metastases), corrected calcium (hypercalcaemia in SCC), EBV/HPV serology if indicated.
- **Imaging:** First-line: CT neck + chest (staging, nodal disease, lung mets). MRI for oral cavity/oropharynx (soft tissue detail). PET-CT for advanced disease (unknown primary, recurrence).
- **Special tests:** Panendoscopy + biopsy under GA (gold standard). HPV/p16 immunohistochemistry on biopsy (prognostic in oropharyngeal SCC). Fine needle aspiration (FNA) of neck node if no primary found.
- **Never-miss test:** **Airway assessment** – flexible nasendoscopy in any patient with stridor or severe dysphagia. Also, cross-sectional imaging (CT) before biopsy if suspected vascular lesion (to avoid catastrophic bleeding).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** Secure airway if stridor – call ENT and anaesthetics; humidified O₂; IV dexamethasone [check local policy, typically 8–16 mg IV stat]; consider helium-O₂ mixture.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):** MDT referral (ENT, oncology, SLT, dietetics). Arrange biopsy & staging. Pain control (simple +/- neuropathic agents). Nutritional assessment: NG tube or PEG if unable to swallow. Dental assessment before radiotherapy (extract septic teeth).
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Radiotherapy ± chemotherapy (cisplatin, cetuximab) or surgery (resection + neck dissection). HPV+ oropharyngeal cancer: de-escalation trials may reduce morbidity. Surveillance: clinical exam + imaging (CT/MRI) at 3–6 monthly intervals.
- **Default Orders:** NBM if biopsy or scan planned; IV fluids (Hartmann's 1 L/8 h if NBM); VTE prophylaxis – LMWH (unless bleeding risk or platelet <50); baseline NEWS2 then 4-hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line pain:** Paracetamol 1 g QDS PO/IV. Morphine 2.5–5 mg PO/IV PRN (start low in older/frail).
- **Neuropathic pain:** Amitriptyline 10 mg PO nocte (increase slowly) or gabapentin [check local policy] (higher starting dose usually 300 mg nocte).
- **Antibiotics (if infection/post-op):** Co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS PO/IV (or clindamycin 300–450 mg QDS PO/IV if penicillin allergic). [Check local policy for doses.]
- **Key contraindications:** Avoid NSAIDs if bleeding risk (thrombocytopenia, anticoagulation, post-op). Caution with opioids in delirium or marginal airway.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Opioid-induced respiratory depression in a patient with pre-existing airway compromise – use smallest effective dose, monitor RR and sedation score. Never give morphine to a patient with stridor without airway backup.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Airway obstruction (tumour/bleed/post-op oedema), haemorrhage (from tumour or carotid blowout), fistula (pharyngocutaneous), aspiration pneumonia, SVC obstruction (large mediastinal mass).
- **Chronic:** Radiation-induced xerostomia (severe dry mouth), osteoradionecrosis of mandible, dysphagia requiring long-term PEG, hypothyroidism (after neck RT), carotid artery stenosis, fibrosis and trismus, second primary tumour.
sections.Prognosis: - Highly variable by site and stage. HPV+ oropharyngeal SCC: 80–90% 5-year survival. HPV- and advanced (Stage III–IV): <50% 5-year survival. Early laryngeal cancer (T1/T2): >90% cure rate. Early detection dramatically improves outcome.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Check for trismus (invasion of pterygoid muscles) – may preclude transoral surgery/radiotherapy positioning.
- Send all oropharyngeal biopsy for HPV/p16 testing – this alters prognosis and sometimes treatment intensity.
- Involve SLT and dietitian **on day of admission** – malnutrition is common and affects outcomes.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do not prescribe NSAIDs in patients at risk of bleeding (thrombocytopenia, post-op, coagulation issues).
- Do not delay airway assessment in the stridorous patient while waiting for imaging – clinical FNE is the priority.
- Do not forget to check for a “sentinel bleed” (small haemoptysis) – this may herald carotid blowout. If present, keep the patient in a monitored bed and alert ENT.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening breathing, voice change, stridor; any bleeding from mouth or neck; difficulty swallowing own saliva; severe pain uncontrolled with oral analgesia; fever or new swelling.
- **Follow-up:** ENT outpatients within 2 weeks (urgent suspicion of cancer pathway). After diagnosis: MDT plan with oncology, SLT, and dietitian.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ENT reg / anaesthetics:** NEWS2 ≥5, stridor at rest, respiratory rate >25, SpO₂ <90% despite O₂, inability to swallow own saliva (near-complete obstruction), any haemoptysis or bleeding from neck.
- **Call crash team / 2222:** Respiratory arrest, massive haemorrhage (carotid blowout – apply direct pressure and call vascular/ENT urgently).

rawMarkdown:
## Head & Neck Cancer — Overview
**One-liner:** SCC of upper aerodigestive tract; smoking/HPV-driven; airway compromise is immediate threat.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway obstruction** from tumour or post-biopsy oedema: listen for stridor, check for biphasic stridor, distress, oxygen saturations. **Single finding:** stridor at rest with inability to lie flat.
- **Carotid blowout** (salvage after radiotherapy/surgery): sentinel bleed, then torrential haemorrhage. **Single finding:** sudden bright red blood from mouth/neck with hypotension.

### Presentation
- Hoarseness >3 weeks, persistent sore throat, dysphagia, odynophagia, referred otalgia (ear pain).
- Neck lump (often cervical lymphadenopathy), non-healing oral ulcer >3 weeks, unilateral nasal obstruction/epistaxis.
- Exam: inspect oral cavity (ulcer, mass, leukoplakia/erythroplakia), palpate neck nodes (fixed/matted), check tongue movement & bite block (trismus), cranial nerves (XII, XI, X — tongue/shoulder/voice), flexible nasendoscopy bedside.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Benign laryngeal lesion** (nodules, papilloma) — bilateral, no ulcer, resolves with voice rest.
- **Infectious pharyngitis/tonsillitis** — acute onset, fever, bilateral exudate, lymphadenopathy tender.
- **Thyroid mass** — midline, moves with swallowing, no mucosal lesion.
- **Lymphoma** — rubbery neck nodes, B symptoms (night sweats, weight loss).
- **Splitter:** Unilateral tonsillar enlargement with ulceration → SCC until proven otherwise; bilateral symmetric enlargement with exudate → infectious.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Flexible nasal endoscopy (FNE) — assess airway patency, lesion site, vocal cord mobility. If stridor → do NOT delay: call ENT/anaesthetics.
- **Bloods:** FBC (anaemia, infection), U&E, LFT (metastases), corrected calcium (hypercalcaemia in SCC), EBV/HPV serology if indicated.
- **Imaging:** First-line: CT neck + chest (staging, nodal disease, lung mets). MRI for oral cavity/oropharynx (soft tissue detail). PET-CT for advanced disease (unknown primary, recurrence).
- **Special tests:** Panendoscopy + biopsy under GA (gold standard). HPV/p16 immunohistochemistry on biopsy (prognostic in oropharyngeal SCC). Fine needle aspiration (FNA) of neck node if no primary found.
- **Never-miss test:** **Airway assessment** – flexible nasendoscopy in any patient with stridor or severe dysphagia. Also, cross-sectional imaging (CT) before biopsy if suspected vascular lesion (to avoid catastrophic bleeding).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** Secure airway if stridor – call ENT and anaesthetics; humidified O₂; IV dexamethasone [check local policy, typically 8–16 mg IV stat]; consider helium-O₂ mixture.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):** MDT referral (ENT, oncology, SLT, dietetics). Arrange biopsy & staging. Pain control (simple +/- neuropathic agents). Nutritional assessment: NG tube or PEG if unable to swallow. Dental assessment before radiotherapy (extract septic teeth).
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Radiotherapy ± chemotherapy (cisplatin, cetuximab) or surgery (resection + neck dissection). HPV+ oropharyngeal cancer: de-escalation trials may reduce morbidity. Surveillance: clinical exam + imaging (CT/MRI) at 3–6 monthly intervals.
- **Default Orders:** NBM if biopsy or scan planned; IV fluids (Hartmann's 1 L/8 h if NBM); VTE prophylaxis – LMWH (unless bleeding risk or platelet <50); baseline NEWS2 then 4-hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line pain:** Paracetamol 1 g QDS PO/IV. Morphine 2.5–5 mg PO/IV PRN (start low in older/frail).
- **Neuropathic pain:** Amitriptyline 10 mg PO nocte (increase slowly) or gabapentin [check local policy] (higher starting dose usually 300 mg nocte).
- **Antibiotics (if infection/post-op):** Co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS PO/IV (or clindamycin 300–450 mg QDS PO/IV if penicillin allergic). [Check local policy for doses.]
- **Key contraindications:** Avoid NSAIDs if bleeding risk (thrombocytopenia, anticoagulation, post-op). Caution with opioids in delirium or marginal airway.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Opioid-induced respiratory depression in a patient with pre-existing airway compromise – use smallest effective dose, monitor RR and sedation score. Never give morphine to a patient with stridor without airway backup.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Airway obstruction (tumour/bleed/post-op oedema), haemorrhage (from tumour or carotid blowout), fistula (pharyngocutaneous), aspiration pneumonia, SVC obstruction (large mediastinal mass).
- **Chronic:** Radiation-induced xerostomia (severe dry mouth), osteoradionecrosis of mandible, dysphagia requiring long-term PEG, hypothyroidism (after neck RT), carotid artery stenosis, fibrosis and trismus, second primary tumour.

### Prognosis
- Highly variable by site and stage. HPV+ oropharyngeal SCC: 80–90% 5-year survival. HPV- and advanced (Stage III–IV): <50% 5-year survival. Early laryngeal cancer (T1/T2): >90% cure rate. Early detection dramatically improves outcome.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Check for trismus (invasion of pterygoid muscles) – may preclude transoral surgery/radiotherapy positioning.
- Send all oropharyngeal biopsy for HPV/p16 testing – this alters prognosis and sometimes treatment intensity.
- Involve SLT and dietitian **on day of admission** – malnutrition is common and affects outcomes.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do not prescribe NSAIDs in patients at risk of bleeding (thrombocytopenia, post-op, coagulation issues).
- Do not delay airway assessment in the stridorous patient while waiting for imaging – clinical FNE is the priority.
- Do not forget to check for a “sentinel bleed” (small haemoptysis) – this may herald carotid blowout. If present, keep the patient in a monitored bed and alert ENT.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening breathing, voice change, stridor; any bleeding from mouth or neck; difficulty swallowing own saliva; severe pain uncontrolled with oral analgesia; fever or new swelling.
- **Follow-up:** ENT outpatients within 2 weeks (urgent suspicion of cancer pathway). After diagnosis: MDT plan with oncology, SLT, and dietitian.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ENT reg / anaesthetics:** NEWS2 ≥5, stridor at rest, respiratory rate >25, SpO₂ <90% despite O₂, inability to swallow own saliva (near-complete obstruction), any haemoptysis or bleeding from neck.
- **Call crash team / 2222:** Respiratory arrest, massive haemorrhage (carotid blowout – apply direct pressure and call vascular/ENT urgently).
